critique.summary: The article discusses the USS Wolverine and USS Sable, two paddle wheel aircraft carriers that operated on Lake Michigan during World War II. These ships, originally luxury passenger liners, were converted into training carriers for naval aviators, including future President George H.W. Bush, and were unique for being coal-powered paddle steamers.
mainPoints: USS Wolverine and USS Sable were paddle wheel aircraft carriers that served on Lake Michigan during WWII.
Both ships were converted from luxury passenger liners that originally operated on the Great Lakes.
They were coal-powered paddle steamers, a propulsion method obsolete in the Navy by that time.
These ships were used primarily for training naval aviators in carrier landings, far from combat zones.
They did not have hangars and stored aircraft on the flight deck, limiting their operational capacity.
Future US President George H.W. Bush trained as a pilot on USS Sable.
The carriers never left the Great Lakes due to size restrictions of the Welland Canal and were never used in combat.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely accurate and supported by historical records. USS Wolverine (ex-Seeandbee) and USS Sable (ex-Greater Buffalo) were indeed paddle wheel steamers converted into training aircraft carriers on the Great Lakes during WWII. Their use as training platforms for carrier pilots, including George H.W. Bush, is well documented. The detail about their coal-powered paddle wheel propulsion is correct and notable as an anomaly in naval aviation history. The article cites a credible source for USS Sable and aligns with known naval history. However, the article lacks multiple sources and perspectives, and some contextual details such as the specific training roles and operational limitations could be expanded for completeness.
considerations: The article does not mention the broader strategic importance of training pilots on inland waters away from enemy threats.
It omits details about the specific types of aircraft used for training on these carriers.
There is no discussion of the eventual fate of these ships after WWII.
The article does not provide multiple sources or perspectives, relying heavily on a single external reference.
It does not clarify the distinction between escort carriers and fleet carriers beyond a brief mention.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion highlights historical and current naval training activities related to Lake Michigan. One comment notes the recovery and preservation of WWII naval fighter planes from the lake, while another mentions ongoing naval training facilities and unusual training ships near Chicago.

comments.author: stockresearcher
comments.text:
An awful lot of the WWII naval fighter planes you see in museums were pulled up from the bottom of Lake Michigan. The pilots training to land on these carriers missed fairly frequently.

The water at the bottom of the lake is great for preservation too, so the planes are usually in very good condition, except for whatever damage occurred when they hit the water.
comments.analysis: This comment provides historically accurate information about WWII naval planes recovered from Lake Michigan, supported by known preservation conditions in freshwater lakes.

comments.author: Animats
comments.text:
The U.S. Navy still has their main training facility near Chicago. And they still have weird training ships. USS Trayer is probably the strangest.[1]

[1] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XQCh1mLTIGk
comments.analysis: This comment correctly references ongoing naval training near Chicago and mentions a specific training ship, adding relevant contemporary context.
